name: Colorado SB 26-189 (Automated Decision-Making Technology)
description: Colorado law establishing consumer notice, disclosure, and human-review rights for automated decision-making technology (ADMT) used in consequential decisions. Repeals and replaces SB 24-205's risk-management/impact-assessment framework (colorado_sb_205, now repealed) with a narrower disclosure-and-rights model: no duty of care against algorithmic discrimination, no deployer risk-management program, no annual impact assessment survive from the predecessor.
verification_note: Verified via web search and a fetch of the Colorado General Assembly's own SB26-189 bill page (https://leg.colorado.gov/bills/sb26-189) plus Norton Rose Fulbright's client summary, 2026-07-10 (Week 3 parallel cleanup). Governor Polis signed SB 26-189 on 2026-05-14. It repeals and reenacts SB 24-205 (colorado_sb_205, framework_version 1.0-sb24-205, now status=repealed with superseded_by=colorado_admt) with a substantively different scheme -- 'automated decision-making technology' (ADMT) and 'consequential decision' replace 'high-risk AI system'; the duty of care against algorithmic discrimination, the deployer risk-management-program requirement, and the annual impact-assessment requirement are all removed entirely (confirmed by Norton Rose Fulbright's summary), not carried forward in any form. This is why the successor is modeled as a new framework_id (colorado_admt) rather than a version bump of colorado_sb_205: the statutory identity, defined terms, and substantive obligations all changed, per the repeal-and-replace test in docs/framework_authoring_guide.md section 7. EFFECTIVE DATE UNRESOLVED: the Colorado General Assembly's own bill page states January 1, 2027 for developer documentation requirements specifically, and suggests August 12, 2026 for 'the act itself' (Colorado's standard effective date for a bill without a safety clause, 90 days after the 2026-05-13 adjournment) -- implying deployer notice/disclosure obligations might bind before developer documentation obligations do. Norton Rose Fulbright's summary states a single 2027-01-01 effective date with no split. The primary enrolled bill text (https://leg.colorado.gov/bill_files/116489/download) could not be fetched directly (exceeds this tool's size limit) to resolve the discrepancy. This dataset conservatively uses 2027-01-01 (the later, more corroborated date, and the one that governs the developer-documentation control this dataset lists) -- NEEDS_OWNER_REVIEW to confirm whether deployer notice/disclosure obligations actually bind as of 2026-08-12 instead, which would matter for any engagement in that window. Regardless of which date is correct, AS OF TODAY (2026-07-10) NEITHER is effective yet: Colorado currently has no comprehensive AI/ADMT statute in force (SB 24-205 repealed before ever taking effect; SB 26-189 not yet effective under either candidate date) -- this is a real, verified finding, not a gap in this dataset. Specific C.R.S. section citations were not confirmed against the primary text and are recorded generically pending that review. Sources: Colorado General Assembly, SB26-189 bill page (https://leg.colorado.gov/bills/sb26-189); Norton Rose Fulbright, 'Colorado enacts revised AI law' (https://www.nortonrosefulbright.com/en-us/knowledge/publications/18733d31/colorado-enacts-revised-ai-law).

covered_admt_consequential_decision.name: Covered ADMT Used in a Consequential Decision

criteria.summary: Automated decision-making technology (ADMT) -- technology that processes personal data and uses computation to generate output (predictions, recommendations, classifications, rankings, or scores) that is used to make, guide, or assist a consequential decision -- concerning an individual's access to, eligibility for, or compensation related to education, employment, housing, financial or lending services, insurance, health care services, or essential government services and public benefits.

consequential_decision_categories.essential_government_service: Essential government services and public benefits

criteria.summary: The system is not ADMT as defined (does not process personal data to generate a qualifying output), or its output is not used to make, guide, or assist a consequential decision in a listed category.
